categories.label: Team & co-founders
categories.summary: Recruit and align people: skills, roles, and lightweight processes before scaling.
activities: Use structured programs (e.g. startup school) and public posts to signal what you are building
Map skill gaps vs. current team; list must-have co-founder roles
Run targeted outreach to potential co-founders (warm intros, communities, matching events)
Define equity split principles and next steps for formalizing team
successCriteria: Co-founder count matches the plan for the next milestone (or solo path is explicit)
Each critical role has an owner or a dated plan to fill
Bonus: industry experience where it matters for your domain

categories.label: Problem research
categories.summary: Validate that the problem is real, painful, and worth solving before you overbuild.
activities: Search communities (e.g. Reddit) and social feeds for how people describe the problem
Join or observe focus groups / forums where your audience gathers
Document who has the problem, severity vs. inconvenience, and current alternatives
Trace root causes; assess how common the problem is in your wedge
Draft a crisp problem statement you can test in conversations
successCriteria: Target customer segment is named (who has the problem)
Problem severity and frequency are evidenced, not assumed
Alternatives and substitutes are listed honestly
A short, testable problem statement exists

categories.label: Audience research
categories.summary: Understand who buys or uses the solution, how they behave, and how to reach them.
activities: Run structured customer interviews (problem, context, past behavior)
Draft a lightweight persona or hypothesis card
Ship a simple landing or waitlist to test interest signals
Analyze characteristics of people who actually face the problem
Test social posts that speak only to the problem (not the product)
successCriteria: A named customer segment with evidence
Top pains and desired outcomes prioritized
Preferred channels to reach them (where they already spend time)

categories.label: Competitor research
categories.summary: Map alternatives, positioning, and whitespace so you build and message differently.
activities: Use market summaries and credible industry sources (where available)
Web search competitors and adjacent tools; capture pricing and positioning
Read community sentiment about competitors and substitutes
Estimate rough share or traction signals only when data exists (avoid fake precision)
Summarize how competitors describe their category and who they target
successCriteria: At least three direct or indirect competitors (or substitutes) named
Default alternatives (manual, spreadsheets, incumbents) documented
Clear differentiation hypothesis for your wedge

categories.label: Build product
categories.summary: Ship the smallest thing that solves the core problem and can be tested with real users.
activities: Draft a PRD or scope doc for the MVP slice
Build in public with lightweight updates (where appropriate)
Keep scope minimal: only what is required to test the riskiest assumption
Maintain a simple timeline with milestones and owners
successCriteria: MVP usable by real users for testing in the target context
PRD or scope doc exists and matches what shipped
Build scope matches the problem statement, not a feature wishlist

categories.label: Finance & funding
categories.summary: Choose path, structure, pricing, and capital basics without over-optimizing too early.
activities: Decide bootstrap vs. raise (hypothesis and next milestones)
Set up banking basics appropriate for your entity stage
Draft a lean pitch deck outline aligned to your story
Pick a pricing model hypothesis and margin sanity check
List relevant accelerators or programs (if raising)
Plan incorporation steps and a simple cap table view as you add people
successCriteria: Funding path chosen (even if provisional) with next decision date
Pricing model hypothesis chosen
Basic filing and banking structure plan aligned with your stage
Cap table reflects founders and early commitments

categories.label: Validate product
categories.summary: Measure behavior, talk to users, and close the loop from feedback to product changes.
activities: Pick one primary KPI (activation, retention, conversion) as the north star for testing
Interview active users; summarize patterns and surprises
Onboard waitlisted users or launch to a small cohort deliberately
Implement a feedback channel and triage into product changes
successCriteria: Primary KPI defined and tracked
Evidence from real users informs the next release
Feedback loop exists (collect → prioritize → ship)

categories.label: Market & GTM
categories.summary: Clarify positioning, channels, and content so the right people find you.
activities: Write a one-line company description
Define three value props tied to audience pains
Pick a small set of channels matched to where your audience is
If content-led: create a 30-day plan per channel at your budget
Set a marketing budget band and brand basics (name, voice, visuals)
successCriteria: One-liner and value props are consistent across surfaces
Channel plan matches audience behavior and budget
Lightweight brand kit or style guide for outbound materials
